skill.name: controlnet-pose
skill.description: Pose-conditioned generation on RunComfy via the `runcomfy` CLI. Routes across Kling 2-6 Motion Control Pro / Standard (transfer the motion / blocking of a reference video onto a target character), community Wan 2-2 Animate (audio-driven character animation with pose conditioning), and Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA (pose-conditioned image generation from an OpenPose / DWPose / canny / depth control image). Picks the right route based on video vs still and stylized vs photoreal. Triggers on "controlnet", "control net", "pose control", "openpose", "DWPose", "transfer pose", "motion control", "pose driven", "character pose", "depth control", "canny edge", "use this pose", or any explicit ask to condition generation on a pose / skeleton / motion / depth / canny reference.
skill.summary: Pose-conditioned image and video generation using RunComfy's ControlNet and motion control models
skill.author: doany-ai

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 76 potential issues, but ALL are false positives after evaluation. The skill uses the legitimate runcomfy CLI for pose-conditioned generation via an external API. The 'backtick' detections are URLs with backtick characters in markdown, not code execution. The 'weak cryptographic' and 'reconnaissance' flags are incorrect pattern matches. Security measures documented include: restricted Bash(runcomfy *) tool access, secure token storage with mode 0600, shell injection prevention via JSON input, and allowlisted outbound endpoints only.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer False Positives - Backtick Characters in URLs
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged 40 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution', but these are backtick characters in markdown URLs (e.g., skills.sh/...) that appear throughout the documentation. No actual Ruby shell execution occurs.

content.user_title: Generate images and videos conditioned on pose references
content.value_statement: Creating characters in specific poses is difficult to describe in text prompts alone. This skill enables pose-conditioned generation by routing to ControlNet models and motion transfer APIs, producing images and videos that accurately follow a provided pose skeleton, depth map, or motion reference.
seo_keywords: Claude Codex Claude Code AI skill
actual_capabilities: Transfer motion from a reference video onto a target character using Kling 2-6 Motion Control
Generate images conditioned on pose skeletons, depth maps, or canny edge references using Z-Image ControlNet LoRA
Route automatically between video pose transfer and image pose conditioning based on input type
Create stylized character animations with Wan 2-2 Animate that respond to pose conditioning
Access ComfyUI workflows for multi-condition ControlNet stacks (pose + depth + canny combined)
limitations: Requires a RunComfy account and API token to function
Cannot generate the control image (pose skeleton, depth map) itself - must be provided externally
Complex multi-condition stacks require manual ComfyUI workflow setup rather than CLI access

use_cases.title: Character design with precise poses
use_cases.description: Artists and designers can generate character illustrations locked to specific poses. Upload a pose skeleton or depth map to control exactly how the character stands, fights, dances, or moves.

use_cases.title: Motion transfer for animation
use_cases.description: Video creators can transfer the motion from a reference video onto a new character. A performance capture or stock video can become an animated mascot or stylized character animation.
use_cases.target_user: Motion graphics artists, video editors, animators

use_cases.title: Consistent character staging for production
use_cases.description: Production teams can maintain pose consistency across multiple shots by using the same pose reference. Each generated image shares the exact blocking and composition.
use_cases.target_user: Game asset creators, marketing teams, production studios

prompt_templates.title: Simple pose-controlled image
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the controlnet-pose skill to generate an image of [character description] in [pose description]. The pose reference is at [control image URL].
prompt_templates.scenario: When user provides a pose skeleton image and wants a character generated in that pose

prompt_templates.title: Video motion transfer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the controlnet-pose skill to transfer the motion from [reference video URL] onto the character in [character image URL]. Use Kling Motion Control Pro.
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants to animate a character following a video reference

prompt_templates.title: Depth-conditioned generation
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the controlnet-pose skill to generate [scene description] using the depth map at [depth URL] for composition control. Style should be [artistic style].
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants depth-based composition control with specific styling

prompt_templates.title: Batch pose variations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the controlnet-pose skill to generate [number] variations of [character description] each using a different pose from [directory path]/*.png. Save outputs to [output directory].
prompt_templates.scenario: When user wants multiple character variations following the same base pose

output_examples.input: Generate a samurai warrior in a battle stance using the pose skeleton at example.com/samurai-pose.png
output_examples.output: A generated image of a samurai character in an aggressive combat stance, matching the pose skeleton exactly. The background is a cherry blossom forest. Style is cinematic.

output_examples.input: Transfer the dance moves from this video to a cartoon mascot character
output_examples.output: A video of the mascot character performing the same dance choreography from the reference video, maintaining the cartoon style while following the motion timing precisely.

best_practices: Use clean, single-subject reference videos for motion transfer - avoid scene cuts or multiple people in the frame
Match the control image type to the model: OpenPose skeletons for pose control, depth maps for depth control, canny images for edge control
Provide high-quality character images with clear silhouettes for best identity preservation during pose transfer
anti_patterns: Do not use multi-person reference videos as the pose source - results will be inconsistent
Do not mix incompatible control types (using a pose skeleton with a depth-control model)
Do not assume the CLI can reach ComfyUI workflows directly - complex stacks require manual workflow setup on the RunComfy web interface

faq.question: What is the difference between motion control and pose-conditioned generation?
faq.answer: Motion control takes a video reference and transfers the motion to a new character. Pose-conditioned generation takes a static image (pose skeleton, depth map, or canny edge) and generates a new image following that structure.

faq.question: Do I need to install anything to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, you need the RunComfy CLI installed (npm i -g @runcomfy/cli) and must run 'runcomfy login' to authenticate with your API token.

faq.question: Can I generate my own pose skeleton images with this skill?
faq.answer: No, the skill does not include pose estimation. You must generate control images (OpenPose, DWPose, depth maps) using separate tools, then host them and pass the URLs.

faq.question: What models are available through this skill?
faq.answer: The skill routes to Kling 2-6 Motion Control (video), Wan 2-2 Animate (video), and Z-Image Turbo ControlNet LoRA (image). More models are available via ComfyUI workflows.

faq.question: Is my uploaded content sent anywhere?
faq.answer: Only to RunComfy's API endpoints (model-api.runcomfy.net). The skill documentation explicitly states no telemetry is sent and only allowlisted RunComfy domains are accessed.

faq.question: How do I handle multi-condition ControlNet stacks (pose + depth + canny)?
faq.answer: The CLI routes support single-condition only. For multi-condition stacks, use the ComfyUI workflows hosted on runcomfy.com/comfyui-workflows through the web interface.
